section: Corrosion of Several Metals in Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
text: Corrosion of Several Metals in Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
CORROSION OF SEVERAL METALS IN SUPERCRITICAL STEAM AT $538^{\circ}C$
The corrosion of several iron- and nickel-base alloys in supercritical steam at 24.1 MPa (3500 psi) and $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has been measured to $7.92 \times 10^{7}$ sec (22,000 hr).
The corrosion rates of low-alloy ferritic steels containing from 1.1 to $8.7\%$ Cr and 0.5 to $1.0\%$ Mo differed by less than a factor of 2 in steam.
Of the alloys studied, the lowest weight gain in $3.6 \times 10^{7}$ sec (10,000 hr) was $0.01\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ for Inconel 718 and the highest $10\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ for the low-alloy ferritic steels.
Thus, our program emphasized Hastelloy N, but included a total of over 80 alloys, mostly iron and nickel base.
The tests were conducted in TVA's Bull Run Steam Plant in supercritical steam at 24.1 MPa (3500 psi) and $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
One test period included test times up to $5.4 \times 10^{7}$ sec (15,000 hr) and the data were reported previously.
A second test period covered an additional $2.52 \times 10^{7}$ sec (7000 hr) on many of the same test coupons and extended the total exposure time to $7.92 \times 10^{7}$ sec (22,000 hr).
This is a coal-fired plant with a supercritical steam cycle and a power generation capability of 980 MW.
The facility is located about 5.49 m (18 ft) upstream from the turbine.
The steam is extremely clean at this location containing less than 1 ppb $\mathsf{O}_2$ , less than 5 ppb Cu, less than 3 ppb Na, less than 15 ppb $\mathsf{SiO}_2$ , and less than 6 ppb Fe.
Hydrazine is added to the feed water to scavenge oxygen, and the pH was controlled at 9.40 to 9.45 with ammonia.
The electrical conductivity of the steam condensate is usually less than $3 \times 10^{-7} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ .
Although the stream was generally very pure, during at least one $1.44 \times 10^{7}$ sec (4000 hr) period the level of impurities was significantly higher.
During this period all specimens gained very close to $0.5\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
1 and a photograph of the disassembled test assembly is shown in Fig.
The steam entered the 4-in.-diam sched 160 type 316 stainless steel test chamber at a flow rate of 0.12 to $0.13\mathrm{kg / sec}$ (16 to 17 lb/min).
The initial sample holder was a cube $50.8\mathrm{mm}$ (2 in.) on each side and would accommodate 140 samples.
A second sample holder was later added that would hold 72 specimens.
Most of the samples were $12.7\mathrm{mm}$ wide $\times 50.8\mathrm{mm}$ long $\times 0.89\mathrm{mm}$ thick (1/2 in. wide $\times$ 2 in. long $\times$ 0.035 in. thick).
Alumina washers $0.51\mathrm{mm}$ (0.020 in.) thick were placed between the specimens before they were bolted in place.
Most of the space between the holder and the vessel was baffled to force flow across the samples at about $6.10\mathrm{m / s}$ (20 fps).
The wall thickness of the reduced section was varied from 0.25 to $0.75\mathrm{mm}$ (0.010 to 0.030 in.) to provide stresses of 531 to $193\mathrm{MPa}$ (77 to $28\times 10^{3}$ psi).
Photograph of the Steam Corrosion Chamber After 19,000 hr of Exposure.
Features to note are the stressed but uninstrumented specimens in the filter (foreground), the two groups of unstressed specimens, and the 10 instrumented stressed specimens.
The stressed specimens have an outside diameter of $25 \mathrm{~mm}$ (1 in.) and a length of $76 \mathrm{~mm}$ (3 in.).
Chemical analyses of the test materials are given in Table 1.
Alloys LC, MC, and NC were small melts of 2 1/4 Cr-1 Mo steel with variations in carbon content, and alloy 72768 is a commercial heat of 2 1/4 Cr-1 Mo steel.
Heats 185 through 237 were 2-lb laboratory melts.
Hastelloy N heats 21541 through 73008 were small 50- to 100-lb melts that were vacuum melted and fabricated by commercial vendors.
Hastelloy N heats 2477, 5065, 5067, 5085, 5095, 5101, and M1566 were large commercial heats of standard Hastelloy N.
All alloys were rolled to $0.89\mathrm{m}$ (0.035 in.) thick sheet.

Samples were sheared, cleaned in acetone and alcohol, and annealed in argon for 1 hr at $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (the low-alloy ferritic steels), 1 hr at $1038^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (the stainless steels), or 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (all other alloys).
The stressed specimens were removed every 1000 hr for examination.
The specimens were weighed on a standard four-place balance with an accuracy of $\pm 0.0001\mathrm{g}$ .
The specimens had surface areas of 12 to $14~\mathrm{cm}^2$ , so the uncertainty in weight corresponded to almost $0.01~\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
The uncertainty in the wall thickness was about $\pm 0.0127 \, \text{mm}$ ( $\pm 0.0005$ in.).
When the nominal wall thickness was $0.254 \, \text{mm}$ (0.010 in.), this uncertainty introduced an error in the stress of $\pm 5\%$ .
When the wall thickness was $0.762 \, \text{mm}$ (0.030 in.), this uncertainty was about $\pm 2\%$ .

Five alloys containing from 1.1 to $8.7\%$ Cr and 0.5 to $1.0\%$ Mo (Table 1) were exposed to steam for 14,000 hr at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and these results were presented previously and are shown in Table A1.
These specimens were exposed 7000 hr, and the data for the second series are shown in Fig.
3 along with the scatterband for the first series.
The extremes of the scatterband for the first series were defined by alloys containing $1.1\%$ Cr (lowest weight gain) and $1.9\%$ Cr (highest weight grain).
After only 1000-hr exposure all alloys except the one containing $8.7\%$ Cr had begun to spall, and after 13,000-hr exposure all alloys had lost weight.[2]
The microstructures of the specimen containing 1.1 and $8.7\%$ Cr were quite similar.
The oxide, which consists of two distinct layers had a total thickness of about $50~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$ .
The microstructure of the metal surface was altered to a depth of about $10~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$ .
Influence of Chromium Content on the Weight Change of Annealed Low-Alloy Ferritic Steels in Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
The weight changes observed with several heats of 2 1/4 Cr-1 Mo steel are shown in Fig.
The scatterbands from the observations of steels containing from 1.1 to $8.7\%$ Cr are shown and the data for the four heats of 2 1/4 Cr-1 Mo steel fall within the scatterbands.
These same alloys were exposed in the $50\%$ cold-worked condition.
5 and 6 shows that cold working did not have a detectable effect on corrosion.

Weight Changes of Several Heats of $21/4$ Cr-1 Mo Steel in Solution-Annealed Condition Exposed to Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $24.1\mathrm{MPa}$ (3500 psi).
Weight Changes of Several Heats of $2 \, \text{l/4}$ Cr-1 Mo Steel in the Cold-Worked Condition Exposed to Steam at $538^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $24.1\text{MPa}$ (3500 psi).
Photomicrographs of three specimens of the 2 l/4 Cr-1 Mo steel are shown in Fig.
The three specimens examined were (1) standard alloy (heat 72768), annealed and air cooled, (2) standard alloy, cold worked $50\%$ , and (3) normal carbon (NC) alloy, annealed and air cooled.
The oxide consisted of two layers and had an overall thickness of about $50~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$ .
The specimen underneath the oxide was affected to a depth of $10~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$ or less.
The variation among the four heats is about the same as the variation noted for duplication specimens of heat 5065.
One of the heats, 2477, was vacuum melted, and the other three were air melted.
After the first 4000-hr exposure, the weight change can be described by an equation of the form
\Delta W = K t ^ {0 \cdot 2 1}, \tag {1}
where $\Delta W$ is the weight gain in $\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , $t$ is the time in hr, and $K$ is a constant.
The perturbation in rate after 15,000 hr is thought to be due to the condenser leaks in the plant and the attendant higher impurity levels in the steam.
Some Hastelloy N specimens from heat 5065 were given various surface treatments before exposure to steam.
Two of these were tested in the as-rolled and solution-treated condition, two others were electropolished, and two were abraded with 400 grit paper before exposure.
Figure 9 shows that the weight changes were least for the electropolished material, intermediate for the as-rolled samples, and greatest for the abraded samples.

(a) and (b) Heat 72768, annealed at $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and air cooled.
(c) and (d) Heat 72768, cold worked $50\%$ .
(e) and (f) Heat NC annealed at $927^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and air cooled.
Weight Changes of Several Heats of Annealed Hastelloy N Exposed to Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
Effect of Surface Finish on the Corrosion of Hastelloy N (Heat 5065) in Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
One specimen was cold worked $50\%$ and tested with an as-rolled surface.
This is in agreement with the results of a prior study in which nickel-base alloys were noted to be less affected by cold working than high chromium steels.[6]
Specimens from small commercial heats of modified Hastelloy N containing from 0.10 to $2.1\%$ Ti were exposed to steam, and the results are summarized in Fig.
Alloys containing $0.1\%$ Ti (21546) and $0.49\%$ Ti (21545) gained weight more rapidly than the standard alloy.
However, the slope (weight change per unit time for greater than 2000 hr) was quite low for both these alloys and about equivalent to that for the standard alloy at long times.
Alloys containing 0.92, 1.30, and $2.1\%$ Ti gained less weight than standard Hastelloy N during 10,000 hr exposure.
However, extrapolations to longer times would indicate that standard Hastelloy N and the alloys containing 0.10 and $0.50\%$ Ti would gain less weight than the alloys containing more titanium.
One alloy containing $0.7\%$ Nb was also exposed and gained less weight than most of the titanium-modified alloys.
Several Alloys of Hastelloy N Modified with Titanium and Niobium and Exposed to Supercritical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
Several laboratory melts of Hastelloy N containing additions of Ti, Al, Zr, Hf, Y, Ce, and Nb were exposed to steam for 22,000 hr, and the weight changes are shown in Fig.
Maximum and minimum weight changes in these alloys differed by less than a factor of 2, but only the alloy containing $1.03\%$ Nb gained less weight than the standard alloy.
The perturbation after 15,000-hr exposure was due to leaking condenser tubes, and the specimen lost weight after the tubes were repaired.
The oxide was not of uniform thickness, but varied from a few to about $20~\mu \mathrm{m}$ thick.
Corrosion of Various Modified Compositions of Hastelloy N in Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
Photomicrographs of Hastelloy N Exposed to Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi) for 22,000 hr.
Photomicrographs of Modified Hastelloy N Exposed to Super-Critical Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).
These steels contain from 5 to $25\%$ Cr, and type 502 stainless steel with $5\%$ Cr has the highest oxidation rate.
Type 17-7 PH steel contains $17\%$ Cr plus $1.15\%$ Al and has the lowest rate of weight change over the test period.
The other four steels have about equal weight changes, but types 406 and 446 seem to be approaching a very low rate of weight gain.
Cold working reduced the corrosion rates of most steels studied by about a factor of 2, but had an even larger effect on type 201 stainless steel.

This reduction of the corrosion rate of high chromium steels has been observed by other investigators.[7-9] The oxides on the annealed and cold-worked type 201 stainless steel specimens were subjected to x-ray diffraction after 6000-hr exposure to steam in an effort to determine a reason for the different oxidation behavior.

The oxide on the annealed specimen consisted of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\alpha\text{-Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ , and the oxide on the cold-worked specimen was $\mathrm{MnFe_2O_4}$ and $\alpha\text{-Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ .

The most striking difference in the two specimens was that the matrix lines for the annealed alloy were those of an fcc cell with a lattice parameter of $3.5975 \pm 0.0006$ A, and those for the cold-worked specimen were those of a bcc cell having a lattice parameter of $2.875 \pm 0.002$ A plus a fcc cell having approximately the same lattice parameter as above.
The bcc lines were not present in an as-cold-worked specimen, so the phase likely formed during exposure to steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Weight Changes of Solution Annealed Stainless Steels in Steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi).

17 the behavior of Armco iron, Monel, Incoloy 800, and several nickel-base alloys is shown.
Monel and Armco iron gained weight at a very high rate whereas Inconel 718 gained weight at a very slow rate.
Inconel 600 fell at an intermediate level of weight gain, and Inconel 601 fell at a lower level.
Incoloy 800 gained weight at a relatively high rate for the first 2000 hr, but the rate rapidly decreased to where the rate was proportional to time to about the one tenth power.
17 and 18, some assessment can be made of the effects of cold working.
Cold working caused Hastelloy B to gain weight at a faster rate and slightly reduced the weight gain of Incoloy 800.
The weight gain of Inconel 718 appeared to be increased slightly by cold working, but the values are still so low that inaccuracies in weighing make this conclusion rather tenuous.

The weight gained in the first 1000 hr was increased by cold working for several of the Hastelloys, but the rate slowed to where weight changes were about equivalent after a few thousand hours for annealed and cold-worked material.
Haynes Alloy 188 gained weight more rapidly in the cold-worked condition than in the annealed condition, but the difference was quite small after 10,000-hr exposure.
Incoloy 800 had a very irregular oxide with the thickness in some areas as much as $10~\mu \mathrm{m}$ .

This is in qualitative agreement with the observations of Tilborg and Linde.[10] Inconel 718 in the annealed condition formed a subsurface reaction product to a depth of about $15~\mu \mathrm{m}$ during exposure to steam, but this product was not present in the material cold worked prior to exposure.

Two heats of standard Hastelloy N tubing (N1 5095 and N2 5101) were evaluated in the stressed condition from 193 to 531 MPa (28.0 to $77.0 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{psi}$ ).
The specimens of both heats in the annealed condition (1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) had shorter rupture times in steam than in argon.

Specimens of heat N1 5095 tested in the as-received condition and stressed below $345 \mathrm{MPa}$ ( $50.0 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{psi}$ ) in steam had rupture times equal to those of specimens tested in argon.
Figure 22 shows the stress-rupture properties of heat N1 5095.

Flaws in the specimens may have contributed to the scatter, but at stresses $<345 \mathrm{MPa}$ ( $<50.0 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{psi}$ ), where wall thicknesses are heavier, there appears to be no significant effect of steam on the rupture time.

Two of the specimens stressed at 193 and $280 \mathrm{MPa}$ (28.0 and $40.6 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{psi}$ ) accumulated 15,000-hr exposure to steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ without rupture and three others at slightly higher stresses accumulated from 10,000 to 11,000 hr of exposure.
Figure 23 shows the stress-rupture properties of heat N2 5101.
The longest rupture time of this heat was 12,000 hr for a specimen stressed at $336 \mathrm{MPa}$ ( $48.8 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{psi}$ ) in steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Rupture Life of Hastelloy N (Heat N1 5095).

The creep rates were calculated from plots of diametral strain, $\Delta D / D$ vs time, which were based on measurements of the internal diameter of the specimens at 1000-hr intervals.
Creep Properties of Hastelloy N at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in Steam Environment.
The three short-term test specimens (1.65 to 75.3 hr) exhibited relatively ductile tearing fracture indicative of a very high strain rate at the moment of fracture.
The specimen from the 2089-hr test had a lower ductility failure.

The corrosion of several alloys in supercritical steam at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 24.1 MPa (3500 psi) was measured for times up to 22,000 hr.

The corrosion rates of five ferritic alloys containing from 1.1 to $8.7\%$ Cr and 0.5 to $1.0\%$ Mo and four heats of 2 1/4 Cr-1 Mo steel containing from 0.009 to $0.12\%$ C were equal to within a factor of 2.
Cold working prior to exposure did not have a detectable effect on the corrosion of 2 l/4 Cr-1 Mo steel.
Although rather large changes were made in the composition of Hastelloy N, the corrosion rate was altered by no more than a factor of 2.
The oxide on Hastelloy N was quite irregular with the thickness in different locations varying from a few to $20~\mu \mathrm{m}$ .
Cold working cause a large reduction in the corrosion rate of type 201 stainless steel.
Inconel 718 had the lowest weight gains, and Incoloy 800 had the lowest corrosion rate with a dependence on (time) $^{0.1}$ .
Inconel 718 in the annealed condition formed a subsurface reaction product to a depth of about $15\mu \mathrm{m}$ , but this product was not present in the cold-worked material.
